# Training Process

-   Training

-   Process orientation

-   Different phases of the training process

-   Models of adaptation

-   Training principles

-   top-down bottom-up approaches

-   interventionism and watchful waiting

<!--# There is the so-called fitness-fatigue model. A model that has established itself as a crucial theoretical framework in sports science for a good 70 years now. It originated from several physiological ideas and observations. Mainly, I think, from the model of homeostasis described by Walter Cannon in the 19th century, and the stress model by Hans Selye, who worked as Cannon's successor in the 1930s and 1940s. Selye's model can be considered a precursor to the fitness-fatigue model. In addition, the discovery of supercompensation, particularly of carbohydrate stores, played a major role. This was possibly discovered quite early by Soviet or East German authors, but was only well-documented according to modern scientific criteria in the “Scandinavian Biopsy Studies”. It was shown there that after the depletion of glycogen in the muscles and a subsequent re-feeding, there is an overshooting reaction of the glycogen stores. The musculature was essentially oversaturated. This supercompensation process, which probably occurs quite similarly for other substrates and physiological processes, was subsequently heavily generalized. It was assumed – and this assumption is still widespread – that all physiological processes in training react similarly. This is where Hans Selye's model comes into play: You stress a system. This leads to a negative deflection of physiological processes (e.g., depletion of substrates or reduction of capacities). Upon returning to homeostasis, the body reacts with an overshooting response to be better prepared for recurring, similar stressors. Eric W. Banister is primarily cited for the fitness-fatigue model. Banister, however, developed this model relatively independently of the purely physiological perspective, rather as a mathematical model. This was a crucial guideline for sports science: Banister did not represent adaptation as a single curve, as was the case with Selye, but as the sum of a negative fatigue curve and a positive fitness curve. He wanted to describe the training process on a mathematical level. A training load leads on the one hand to a short-term reduction in performance, as fatigue increases, but on the other hand also to an improvement in fitness. Both fitness and fatigue show an exponential return to baseline. However, in the original model, fatigue dissipates much faster than fitness – I believe Banister described the decline of fatigue in about a third of the time it takes for fitness to dissipate. This creates a window of opportunity in which another training stimulus can be applied. The optimal time is when fatigue is already low again, but the fitness level is still relatively high. At this point, the overall performance capacity is higher than at the starting point. The problem with the fitness-fatigue model – which was already outlined over 20 years ago in the frequently cited paper by Chiu and Barnes (2003) – is obvious: The idea that all bodily functions can be reduced to just two curves is highly oversimplified. Newer approaches attempt to break down this simplification. The rationale is that both fatigue and fitness must be described by numerous physiological processes. “Fatigue” could mean the fatigue of the musculature or other parts of the musculoskeletal system (tendons, ligaments, bones, nerves). It could be the reduction of enzymes or necessary neurotransmitters, a change in pH value, or processes involved in the reuptake of electrolytes. We cannot possibly assume that all these processes return to their baseline in the exact same timeframe. [Slide 11] How do we react to this? What do we do to ensure that we are still training the specific competence we want to improve at exactly the right time in the training process? There are basically two solutions: periodization and monitoring (including intervening in the training process). Periodization is an a priori assumption where we divide the training content into regular cycles to allow for the full recovery of all these different “curves”. Monitoring and the accompanying training control, on the other hand, serve as reactive methods to continuously adjust the training process.-->
